text: In this video, we will present the robotic treatment of a patient with hemorrhagic syndrome type V. A sixteen-year-old female patient with a medical history of rheumatoid arthritis and atrial fibrillation was diagnosed with hemorrhagic syndrome. ERCP confirmed the presence of a cholecystocolonic fistula, thus confirming the presence of a type V-A SENDES-MEDICI syndrome. We decided to use the robotic approach using the DAVINCI XI surgical system.

Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon encounters severe inflammatory adhesions between the colon and gallbladder. Using monopolar scissors, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully mobilizing the colon from the fibrotic and sclerotic gallbladder. A plane is opened between the colon and gallbladder, allowing further mobilization of the colon from the liver and hepatoduodenal ligament. The surgeon prepares to close the fistula between the colon and gallbladder by first closing the opening in the colon.

The surgeon manually closes a large opening in the transverse colon using a technique similar to Heineken Mikulić Structuroplasty. Two corner stitches with V-Lock II-O are placed, followed by a running suture of the inner layer using V-Lock III-O barbed sutures. The outer layer is completed with previously placed stitches, and the closure is finalized by tying the outer layer stitches.

The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, encountering difficulty in finding a clear plane between the gallbladder and liver, leading to a wedge resection of liver segments IVb and Vb. The gallbladder is sent for pathology, but no malignancy is detected in the frozen section. The hepatoduodenal ligament is dissected to isolate the common bile duct, which is opened anteriorly at the fistula site between the gallbladder and common bile duct. The posterior wall is transected, allowing extraction of a large stone from the distal common bile duct. The distal common bile duct is isolated and closed using a stapler due to its large caliber.

The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. The omentum is partially split, and the small bowel is transected approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. The small bowel is lifted in an anti-mesocolic fashion, and an end-to-side hepaticojejunostomy is performed. A posterior layer is sutured using a V-lock IV-O absorbable suture, followed by the creation of a small enterotomy. The anterior layer is then sutured, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon fixes the sutures with a hemolog clip and restores FENST by creating an enteroenterostomy approximately sixty centimeters distal to the hepatic otogenostomy. The posterior layer of the anastomosis is constructed using a VLOG-IIO absorbable wire in a running fashion. The small bowel is opened on both sides, and an inner layer is created with a VLOG-O absorbable wire, completed in a running fashion. Another VLOG-O is used for the outer layer on the anterior side, completing the anastomosis.
